From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

End of a river where it flows into a larger body of water

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Nile_River_and_delta_from_orbit.jpg/250px-Nile_River_and_delta_from_orbit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nile_River_and_delta_from_orbit.jpg)

The mouth of the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") where it empties into the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Desembocadura_del_Ebro.jpg/250px-Desembocadura_del_Ebro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Desembocadura_del_Ebro.jpg)

The mouth of the [Ebro](/wiki/Ebro "Ebro")

A **river mouth** is where a [river](/wiki/River "River") flows into a larger [body of water](/wiki/Body_of_water "Body of water"), such as another river, a [lake](/wiki/Lake "Lake")/[reservoir](/wiki/Reservoir "Reservoir"), a [bay](/wiki/Bay "Bay")/[gulf](/wiki/Gulf "Gulf"), a [sea](/wiki/Sea "Sea"), or an [ocean](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean").[[1]](#cite_note-Hogg-1) At the river mouth, [sediments](/wiki/Sediments "Sediments") are often deposited due to the slowing of the current, reducing the carrying capacity of the water.[[1]](#cite_note-Hogg-1)

The water from a river can enter the receiving body in a variety of different ways.[[1]](#cite_note-Hogg-1) The motion of a river is influenced by the relative density of the river compared to the receiving water, the rotation of the Earth, and any ambient motion in the receiving water, such as [tides](/wiki/Tides "Tides") or [seiches](/wiki/Seiches "Seiches").[[2]](#cite_note-Ma-2)

If the river water has a higher [density](/wiki/Density "Density") than the surface of the receiving water, the river water will plunge below the surface. The river water will then either form an underflow or an [interflow](/wiki/Interflow "Interflow") within the lake. However, if the river water is lighter than the receiving water, as is typically the case when fresh river water flows into the sea, the river water will float along the surface of the receiving water as an overflow.

Alongside these [advective](/wiki/Advection "Advection") transports, inflowing water will also [diffuse](/wiki/Diffuse "Diffuse").[[1]](#cite_note-Hogg-1)

## Landforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=River_mouth&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Landforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Kinburn_Spit_by_Darkngs.jpg/250px-Kinburn_Spit_by_Darkngs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kinburn_Spit_by_Darkngs.jpg)

Aerial picture of the Kinburn Spit.

At the mouth of a river, the change in flow conditions can cause the river to drop any sediment it is carrying. This [sediment](/wiki/Sediment "Sediment") deposition can generate a variety of [landforms](/wiki/Landform "Landform"), such as [deltas](/wiki/River_delta "River delta"), [sand bars](/wiki/Sand_bar "Sand bar"), [spits](/wiki/Spit_(landform) "Spit (landform)"), and tie channels.[[3]](#cite_note-Rowland-3) Landforms at the river mouth drastically alter the geomorphology and ecosystem. Along coasts, sand bars and similar landforms act as barriers, sheltering sensitive ecosystems that are enriched by nutrients deposited from the river.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) However, [the damming](/wiki/Damming "Damming") of rivers can starve the river of sand and nutrients, creating a deficit at the river's mouth.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

## Cultural influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=River_mouth&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cultural influence")]

As river mouths are the site of large-scale sediment deposition and allow for easy travel and ports, many towns and cities are founded there. Many places in the United Kingdom take their names from their positions at the mouths of rivers, such as Plymouth (i.e. mouth of the [Plym River](/wiki/River_Plym "River Plym")), Sidmouth (i.e. mouth of the [Sid River](/wiki/River_Sid "River Sid")), and Great Yarmouth (i.e. mouth of the [Yare River](/wiki/River_Yare "River Yare")). In Celtic, the corresponding terms are [Aber or Inver](/wiki/Aber_and_Inver_(placename_elements) "Aber and Inver (placename elements)"), from which come numerous place names such as [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen "Aberdeen"), [Abercrombie](/wiki/Abercrombie,_Fife "Abercrombie, Fife"), and [Abernethy](/wiki/Abernethy,_Perth_and_Kinross "Abernethy, Perth and Kinross"), as well as [Inverness](/wiki/Inverness "Inverness"), [Inverkip](/wiki/Inverkip "Inverkip"), and [Inveraray](/wiki/Inveraray "Inveraray").

Due to rising sea levels as a result of climate change, the coastal cities are at heightened risk of flooding. Sediment starvation in the river compounds this concern.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)
